solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1571J - Two Railroads
Firstly, the problem asks us to find maximum flow in some special network. Instead of the maximum flow, we will try to find the minimum cut for each query.
Recall that an $$$s$$$-$$$t$$$-cut in a flow network is a partition of its vertices into two sets, $$$S$$$ and $$$T$$$, such that $$$s \in S$$$, $$$t \in T$$$, and the total capacity of all arcs leading from a vertex in $$$S$$$ to a vertex in $$$T$$$ is the value of the cut. The maximum flow from $$$s$$$ to $$$t$$$ is equal to the minimum $$$s$$$-$$$t$$$-cut.
Let's analyze the properties of the cut in this network. For simplicity, I will denote the $$$i$$$-th Main station as the vertex $$$v_i$$$, and the $$$i$$$-th Auxiliary station as the vertex $$$u_i$$$.
First of all, if we have a cut where $$$v_i \in S$$$ and $$$v_{i-1} \in T$$$, this cut is not a minimum one. If $$$u_{i - 1} \in S$$$, then we can add $$$v_{i-1}$$$ into $$$S$$$ and remove it from $$$T$$$, so the value of the cut will decrease at least by $$$a_{i-1}$$$. If $$$u_i \notin S$$$, we can remove $$$v_i$$$ from $$$S$$$ and decrease the value of the cut at least by $$$c_i$$$. If both $$$u_i$$$ and $$$v_i$$$ belong to $$$S$$$, and both $$$u_{i-1}$$$ and $$$v_{i-1}$$$ belong to $$$T$$$, we can just remove both $$$u_i$$$ and $$$v_i$$$ from $$$S$$$, and the value of the cut won't increase. So, it means that we can search for a cut where some prefix of vertices on the Main road belongs to $$$S$$$. Similarly, some prefix of vertices on the Auxiliary road will belong to $$$S$$$.
Now there are four cases how the cut can go. They are described in the following pictures:
Let's analyze these cases one by one. For simplicity, denote $$$p_i = \sum\limits_{j=1}^{i} c_j$$$.
Case 1: if the last $$$v$$$-vertex in $$$S$$$ is $$$v_x$$$, then the value of the cut is $$$a_x + c_l + c_{l+1} + \dots + c_{x-1} + c_x$$$, which can be rewritten as $$$a_x + p_x - p_{l-1}$$$. The minimum value can be calculated with a RMQ query of the value $$$a_x + p_x$$$ on the segment $$$[l, r - 1]$$$, since $$$p_{l-1}$$$ is constant.
Case 2: if the last $$$v$$$-vertex in $$$S$$$ is $$$v_x$$$, then the value of the cut is $$$a_x + c_{x+1} + c_{x+2} + \dots + c_r$$$, which can be rewritten as $$$a_x + p_r - p_x$$$. The minimum value can be calculated with a RMQ query of the value $$$a_x - p_x$$$ on the segment $$$[l, r - 1]$$$, since $$$p_r$$$ is constant.
Case 3: if the last $$$v$$$-vertex in $$$S$$$ is $$$v_x$$$, and the last $$$u$$$-vertex in $$$S$$$ is $$$u_y$$$, the value of the cut is $$$a_x + b_y + c_{y+1} + c_{y+2} + \dots + c_x = a_x + p_x - b_y - p_y$$$. We need to look deeper into this case, there's no clear way to solve this right now.
Case 4: similar to Case 3, if we can solve that case, we'll solve Case 4.
Let's analyze the Case 3 more thoroughly. We have to minimize the value of $$$a_x + p_x - b_y - p_y$$$ under the constraints $$$l \le y \le x \le r$$$. Instead of this, let's lift one of the constraints; let's say that $$$y$$$ can be less than $$$l$$$. Then we can easily calculate the minimum value of $$$a_x + p_x - b_y - p_y$$$: for every $$$x$$$, precalculate the minimum $$$b_y + p_y$$$ to the left of it and "match" them; then just use RMQ on the precalculated values.
But doesn't the fact that we have lifted one of the constraints make our solution wrong? In fact, it doesn't. Suppose the answer to the Case 3 is "illegal" because the value of $$$y$$$ we found is less than $$$l$$$. Let's take a look at the picture of this case and the Case 2:
In fact, if Case 3 yields us an illegal solution, it will be overwritten by the solution of Case 2 since an illegal cut for the Case 3 can be transformed into a legal cut for Case 2 (and the value of the cut will only decrease). Similarly, illegal solutions of Case 4 (where we'll lift the constraint $$$y \le r$$$) can be fixed by Case 1.
So, we can answer each query with just four RMQ queries, and our solution runs in, for example, $$$O(n + q \log n)$$$ if we use a segment tree to process RMQ queries.
